summary: 19th-century English philosopher, the foremost developer of utilitarianism after Bentham. Author of On Liberty (harm principle, tyranny of the majority), Utilitarianism (higher/lower pleasures, greatest happiness principle), The Subjection of Women (co-written with Harriet Taylor), and A System of Logic (five methods of induction, theory of names).

works.name: On Liberty & the Harm Principle
works.description: Mill's most famous political philosophy work (1859), co-written with his wife Harriet Taylor. It introduces the 'harm principle' — that power can only be rightfully exercised over an individual to prevent harm to others. The work warns against the 'tyranny of the majority' (quoting Tocqueville), uses Marcus Aurelius as an example of a well-intentioned persecutor, and discusses a Muslim country banning pork to show how morals can influence decisions. It contrasts 'Christian self-denial' with 'Pagan self-assertion.'

clues.clue: harm principle — power should only restrict individual freedom to prevent harm to others
examples: came up with the 'harm principle' as a guideline for when the state should restrict freedom
personal behavior should only be regulated according to the 'harm principle'

clues.clue: tyranny of the majority — democracy can oppress minorities (quotes Tocqueville)
examples: opens by warning against the oppression of minorities via 'tyranny of the majority'

works.name: Utilitarianism (the essay)
works.description: Mill's 1861 essay defending and refining the greatest happiness principle. It distinguishes between higher and lower pleasures ('better to be Socrates dissatisfied than a fool satisfied'), rebutting the charge that utilitarianism is a 'doctrine worthy only of swine.' The essay contains a notorious 'proof' comparing visibility to desirability, which G.E. Moore cited as a case of the naturalistic fallacy. It credits the term 'utilitarian' to John Galt's Annals of the Parish.

clues.clue: higher vs. lower pleasures — 'better to be Socrates dissatisfied than a fool satisfied'
examples: distinguished between higher and lower pleasures
better to be Socrates dissatisfied than a fool satisfied

clues.clue: defended against 'doctrine worthy only of swine' charge
examples: denies teaching a 'doctrine worthy only of swine'
this thinker defended a theory of ethics against claims it was a 'doctrine worthy only of swine'

clues.clue: notorious 'proof' — analogy between visible/desirable; G.E. Moore cited as naturalistic fallacy
examples: G.E. Moore claimed that this thinker made a clear error when using the word 'desirable' like the word 'audible'
a 'proof' analogizes how we can only know 'that an object is visible' because 'people actually see it'

clues.clue: push-pin — Bentham claimed quantity of pleasure being equal, push-pin is as good as poetry; Mill disagreed
examples: Bentham famously claimed that 'quantity of pleasure being equal' this card game is as good as poetry

works.name: The Subjection of Women & Feminism
works.description: Mill's 1869 essay on gender equality, co-written with his wife Harriet Taylor. It argues for female education and suffrage, claims 'there remain no legal slaves, save the mistress of every house,' and attacks marriage laws. Harriet Taylor also wrote The Enfranchisement of Women independently. Mill served in Parliament and sponsored the 1868 Married Women's Property Bill.

attacked the armchair physiology used to justify preventing the title group from trying things

works.name: A System of Logic & Theory of Names
works.description: Mill's 1843 work on scientific methodology and the philosophy of language. It presents five methods of induction (agreement, difference, concomitant variations, residues, and joint method of agreement and difference) and Mill's direct reference theory of names (Millianism), which holds that a name's meaning is only its referent. The Dartmouth example shows a name can persist even when its original descriptive connection is severed. Saul Kripke rejected Mill's theory of general terms but resurrected his theory of proper names. The sixth book, 'On the Logic of the Moral Sciences,' was influential in establishing social science.

clues.clue: five methods of induction: agreement, difference, concomitant variations, residues, joint method
examples: introduced concomitant variations, residues, difference, and agreement as 'methods of experimental inquiry'
this thinker demonstrated the direct method of agreement involving circumstances 'A B C' as the first of five methods

works.name: Principles of Political Economy
works.description: Mill's widely used economics textbook, largely replaced by Alfred Marshall's Principles of Economics at the turn of the 20th century. It advocates worker cooperatives as the 'nearest approach to social justice,' discusses the 'Stationary State' and warns against unlimited increases in wealth and population. It reflects Mill's utilitarian views and includes a chapter 'On the Probable Futurity of the Working Classes.'

clues.clue: advocated worker cooperatives / industrial cooperatives as 'nearest approach to social justice'
examples: proposed inculcating democratic values among workers by making firms industrial cooperatives
advocated for common ownership of raw materials

examples: a textbook by this man was largely replaced by Alfred Marshall's Principles of Economics

examples: that author with this surname advocated for common ownership of raw materials in a chapter titled 'On the Probable Futurity of the Working Classes'

clues.clue: 'reciprocal demand' — how elasticity of demand influences terms of trade
examples: this economist introduced the phrase 'reciprocal demand'

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Mill was raised in near-total isolation from other children and taught Greek from age three by his father James Mill, who wrote Elements of Political Economy and The History of British India. He suffered a mental breakdown at age twenty. He paired Bentham with Coleridge as the 'seminal minds' of their age. He debated Thomas Carlyle over slavery (Carlyle's 'Occasional Discourse on the Negro Question' coined the term 'dismal science'). Mill justified colonialism by not applying the harm principle to 'uncivilized' communities. He defended the death penalty in Parliament as the 'least cruel mode of punishment.'

comprehensive_summary:
John Stuart Mill is one of the most heavily clued philosophers in quizbowl, with major works spanning ethics, political philosophy, logic, economics, and feminism. His most famous work, On Liberty (1859), co-written with his wife Harriet Taylor, introduces the 'harm principle' — the idea that power can only be rightfully exercised over an individual to prevent harm to others. The work warns against the 'tyranny of the majority' (quoting Tocqueville), uses Marcus Aurelius as an example of well-intentioned persecution, and discusses a Muslim country banning pork to illustrate how morals influence decisions.

Mill's 1861 essay Utilitarianism refines Bentham's greatest happiness principle by distinguishing between higher and lower pleasures, famously claiming 'better to be Socrates dissatisfied than a fool satisfied.' The essay contains a notorious 'proof' comparing visibility to desirability, which G.E. Moore cited as a case of the naturalistic fallacy. It also credits the term 'utilitarian' to John Galt's novel Annals of the Parish.

The Subjection of Women (1869), co-written with Harriet Taylor, argues for female education and suffrage, claiming 'there remain no legal slaves, save the mistress of every house.' Harriet Taylor independently wrote The Enfranchisement of Women. Mill served in Parliament and sponsored the 1868 Married Women's Property Bill.

A System of Logic (1843) presents five methods of induction (agreement, difference, concomitant variations, residues, and joint method) and Mill's direct reference theory of names (Millianism), which holds that a name's meaning is only its referent — illustrated by the Dartmouth example. Saul Kripke rejected Mill's theory of general terms but resurrected his theory of proper names. The sixth book, 'On the Logic of the Moral Sciences,' was influential for social science methodology.

Mill's Principles of Political Economy was a widely used economics textbook that advocated worker cooperatives and was eventually replaced by Alfred Marshall's work. Mill was raised in near-total isolation from other children, taught Greek from age three by his father James Mill, and suffered a mental breakdown at age twenty. He debated Thomas Carlyle over slavery, justified colonialism by not applying the harm principle to 'barbarous' peoples, and defended the death penalty in Parliament.
